title: Triple-Phase Multimodal Knowledge Aggregation Framework for Microbial Keratitis Subtype Diagnosis on Slit-Lamp Photography
summary: Researchers led by Yiqing Wang at the University of Michigan developed a triple-phase multimodal AI framework that classifies bacterial versus fungal microbial keratitis using slit-lamp photographs captured under three illumination modes (blue-light, sclerotic-scatter, and white-light) combined with clinical metadata. The model leverages cross-modality contrastive learning, modality-specific fine-tuning, and feature-level multimodal ensemble learning to produce patient-level predictions. Evaluated on a multicenter cohort of 1,645 patients and 17,158 images from India and the United States, the system achieved 85.84% accuracy, an 84.46% average F1-score, and a 0.885 AUC. The authors caution that pooled aggregate results were overly optimistic compared with resampling- and balance-based re-evaluation, highlighting real challenges in cross-site generalization. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/yqwang01/TPMKA; the dataset will be shared pending University of Michigan data-sharing clearance.
analysis_notes: Analysis based entirely on the arXiv abstract page; individual metric breakdowns (e.g., per-class sensitivity/specificity for bacterial vs. fungal) and comparison baselines against existing clinical methods are not available without accessing the full PDF.
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03740#content) ![](https://arxiv.org/static/base/1.0.1/images/icons/smileybones-small.svg)arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! [Learn more](https://info.arxiv.org/about) × Search arXiv Press Enter to search · [Advanced search](https://arxiv.org/search/advanced) # Quantitative Biology > Quantitative Methods **arXiv:2607.03740** (q-bio) \[Submitted on 4 Jul 2026 ( [v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03740v1)), last revised 8 Jul 2026 (this version, v2)\] # Title:Triple-Phase Multimodal Knowledge Aggregation Framework for Microbial Keratitis Subtype Diagnosis on Slit-Lamp Photography Authors: [Yiqing Wang](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Wang,+Y), [Maria A. Woodward](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Woodward,+M+A), [Ziyun Yang](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Yang,+Z), [N. Venkatesh Prajna](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Prajna,+N+V), [Chunming He](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=He,+C), [Leslie M. Niziol](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Niziol,+L+M), [Mercy Pawar](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Pawar,+M), [Ming-Chen Lu](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Lu,+M), [Guillermo Amescua](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Amescua,+G), [Rachel Wozniak](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Wozniak,+R), [Sejal Amin](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Amin,+S), [Abinaya Krishnan](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Krishnan,+A), [Prabhleen Kochar](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Kochar,+P), [Sina Farsiu](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Farsiu,+S) View a PDF of the paper titled Triple-Phase Multimodal Knowledge Aggregation Framework for Microbial Keratitis Subtype Diagnosis on Slit-Lamp Photography, by Yiqing Wang and 13 other authors [View PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.03740) > Abstract:Microbial keratitis requires rapid pathogen identification to guide treatment, but culture- and PCR-based diagnostics are slow and resource-intensive. We developed a triple-phase multimodal framework for bacterial-versus-fungal keratitis classification using slit-lamp photographs acquired under blue-light, sclerotic-scatter, and white-light illumination, together with clinical metadata. The model combines cross-modality contrastive learning, modality-specific fine-tuning, and feature-level multimodal ensemble learning for patient-level prediction. We evaluated the framework on a multicenter dataset of 1,645 patients and 17,158 images from India and the United States. The model achieved 85.84% accuracy, 84.46% average F1-score, and 0.885 AUC. Site-specific evaluation showed that pooled results were overly optimistic, whereas resampling- and balance-based re-evaluation provided a more realistic assessment of cross-site generalization. Under all settings, our framework remained the top-performing approach. The code is available at [this https URL](https://github.com/yqwang01/TPMKA) and dataset access will be provided subject to University of Michigan data-sharing clearance. | | | | --- | --- | | Subjects: | Quantitative Methods (q-bio.QM); Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (cs.CV); Image and Video Processing (eess.IV) | | Cite as: | [arXiv:2607.03740](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03740) \[q-bio.QM\] | | | (or [arXiv:2607.03740v2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.03740v2) \[q-bio.QM\] for this version) | | | [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.03740](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.03740)

title: Esports and Physiological Tremor a StarCraft 2 Tournament Study
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06577#content) ![](https://arxiv.org/static/base/1.0.1/images/icons/smileybones-small.svg)arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! [Learn more](https://info.arxiv.org/about) × Search arXiv Press Enter to search · [Advanced search](https://arxiv.org/search/advanced) # Quantitative Biology > Quantitative Methods **arXiv:2607.06577** (q-bio) \[Submitted on 2 Jul 2026\] # Title:Esports and Physiological Tremor a StarCraft 2 Tournament Study Authors: [Andrzej Białecki](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Bia%C5%82ecki,+A), [Izabela Ghafour](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Ghafour,+I), [Szymon Kuliś](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Kuli%C5%9B,+S), [Maciej Skorulski](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Skorulski,+M), [Jan Gajewski](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Gajewski,+J) View a PDF of the paper titled Esports and Physiological Tremor a StarCraft 2 Tournament Study, by Andrzej Bia{\\l}ecki and 4 other authors [View PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.06577) [HTML (experimental)](https://arxiv.org/html/2607.06577v1) > Abstract:Physiological tremor of the upper limb is a sensitive neuromuscular indicator that may be modulated by cognitive load and competitive stress, yet its behaviour in real esports conditions remains uncharacterised. We measured wrist accelerometer-based tremor in 16 healthy adult male StarCraft~2 players across two tournament days, computing log power spectral density (log(PSD)) and dominant frequency in four bands (2--4, 8--14, 10--20, and 1--25Hz) and comparing them to published population norms using linear mixed models. Players deviated significantly from the reference in all bands: log(PSD) was elevated at 2--4~Hz and substantially reduced at higher frequencies (Cohen's d=1.6--2.3), suggesting long-term neuromuscular adaptation to the fine-motor demands of esports. Tremor indicators declined systematically over the tournament day. Contrary to the fatigue-related increases typical of traditional motor tasks. Neither game outcome nor actions per minute significantly predicted post-game tremor. These findings suggest physiological tremor may reflect a generalised psychophysiological adaptation to competitive esports rather than being a short-term performance predictor. | | | | --- | --- | | Comments: | 11 pages, 3 Figures, 4 Tables | | Subjects: | Quantitative Methods (q-bio.QM); Human-Computer Interaction (cs.HC) | | ACM classes: | H.5.0 | | Cite as: | [arXiv:2607.06577](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06577) \[q-bio.QM\] | | | (or [arXiv:2607.06577v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06577v1) \[q-bio.QM\] for this version) | | | [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.06577](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.06577)

title: Polynomial encoding of rooted trees with branch lengths
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06591#content) ![](https://arxiv.org/static/base/1.0.1/images/icons/smileybones-small.svg)arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! [Learn more](https://info.arxiv.org/about) × Search arXiv Press Enter to search · [Advanced search](https://arxiv.org/search/advanced) # Quantitative Biology > Populations and Evolution **arXiv:2607.06591** (q-bio) \[Submitted on 6 Jul 2026\] # Title:Polynomial encoding of rooted trees with branch lengths Authors: [Pengyu Liu](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Liu,+P), [Joan Carles Pons](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Pons,+J+C), [Gerard Ribas](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Ribas,+G) View a PDF of the paper titled Polynomial encoding of rooted trees with branch lengths, by Pengyu Liu and 2 other authors [View PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.06591) [HTML (experimental)](https://arxiv.org/html/2607.06591v1) > Abstract:Phylogenetic trees are rooted trees with branch lengths that record genetic divergence or elapsed time, and quantifying differences between them is central to a wide range of evolutionary and epidemiological analyses. Graph-polynomial encodings of rooted trees provide an accurate, interpretable, and computationally efficient way to compare tree shapes, but existing polynomial encodings must be paired with auxiliary structures to study rooted trees with branch lengths. We introduce a bivariate polynomial encoding that incorporates branch lengths directly into a recursive computation from the leaf vertices to the root vertex of a tree. We prove that, for rooted trees with branch lengths and no vertices of degree two, which include all standard phylogenetic trees, two trees have the same polynomial if and only if their underlying unlabeled trees are isomorphic and the branch lengths of corresponding edges are equal. We apply the polynomial encoding to three published HIV-1 phylogenies sampled in different epidemiological settings and show that it accurately separates the three datasets based on their tree topologies and branch lengths, outperforming previous polynomial-based approaches for analyzing rooted trees with branch lengths. | | | | --- | --- | | Comments: | 11 pages, 3 figures | | Subjects: | Populations and Evolution (q-bio.PE); Combinatorics (math.CO) | | MSC classes: | 05C05, 05C31, 92B10, 05C60, 92D15, 05C90, 05C22 | | Cite as: | [arXiv:2607.06591](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06591) \[q-bio.PE\] | | | (or [arXiv:2607.06591v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.06591v1) \[q-bio.PE\] for this version) | | | [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.06591](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.06591)

title: Dimension-dependent continuum limits in tissue mechanics
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07000#content) ![](https://arxiv.org/static/base/1.0.1/images/icons/smileybones-small.svg)arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! [Learn more](https://info.arxiv.org/about) × Search arXiv Press Enter to search · [Advanced search](https://arxiv.org/search/advanced) # Nonlinear Sciences > Cellular Automata and Lattice Gases **arXiv:2607.07000** (nlin) \[Submitted on 8 Jul 2026\] # Title:Dimension-dependent continuum limits in tissue mechanics Authors: [Matthew J Simpson](https://arxiv.org/search/nlin?searchtype=author&query=Simpson,+M+J), [Pascal R Buenzli](https://arxiv.org/search/nlin?searchtype=author&query=Buenzli,+P+R) View a PDF of the paper titled Dimension-dependent continuum limits in tissue mechanics, by Matthew J Simpson and Pascal R Buenzli [View PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.07000) [HTML (experimental)](https://arxiv.org/html/2607.07000v1) > Abstract:Continuum descriptions of epithelial tissue mechanics can replace expensive individual-based simulations with tractable macroscopic models, yet the link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport remains poorly understood. We show that dimensionality controls this link: long-time mechanical relaxation rates reveal generalized porous-media-type nonlinear transport phenomena, D(ρ)∝ργ. Exponents in nonlinear diffusivities are fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality, providing a novel physical mechanism for emergent macroscopic transport phenomena. | | | | --- | --- | | Comments: | 3 Figures, 16 Pages | | Subjects: | Cellular Automata and Lattice Gases (nlin.CG); Biological Physics (physics.bio-ph); Computational Physics (physics.comp-ph); Tissues and Organs (q-bio.TO) | | MSC classes: | 35Q92, 35Q70 | | Cite as: | [arXiv:2607.07000](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07000) \[nlin.CG\] | | | (or [arXiv:2607.07000v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07000v1) \[nlin.CG\] for this version) | | | [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.07000](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.07000)

title: Enhanced sampling and cryo-EM data resolve magnesium binding to RNA
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07186#content) ![](https://arxiv.org/static/base/1.0.1/images/icons/smileybones-small.svg)arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! [Learn more](https://info.arxiv.org/about) × Search arXiv Press Enter to search · [Advanced search](https://arxiv.org/search/advanced) # Quantitative Biology > Biomolecules **arXiv:2607.07186** (q-bio) \[Submitted on 8 Jul 2026\] # Title:Enhanced sampling and cryo-EM data resolve magnesium binding to RNA Authors: [Olivier Languin-Cattoën](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Languin-Catto%C3%ABn,+O), [Elisa Posani](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Posani,+E), [Giovanni Bussi](https://arxiv.org/search/q-bio?searchtype=author&query=Bussi,+G) View a PDF of the paper titled Enhanced sampling and cryo-EM data resolve magnesium binding to RNA, by Olivier Languin-Catto\\"en and 1 other authors [View PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.07186) > Abstract:Magnesium ions are essential for RNA structure but difficult to model due to slow binding kinetics and experimental limitations. We present an enhanced-sampling strategy that accelerates Mg2+ inner-shell binding by orders of magnitude, enabling quantitative exploration of ion-binding motifs in a large ribozyme. The method combines a barrier-flattening bias with Hamiltonian replica exchange to efficiently sample multiple equivalent binding sites, and builds on an approach that achieved top performance in the CASP16 blind assessment of RNA solvation structure. Using cryo-electron microscopy maps for validation, we introduce a local analysis framework that infers the population of individual binding motifs from their agreement with experimental density, enabling site-by-site validation. We find that insufficient sampling of inner-shell binding leads to significantly poorer agreement with experiment, whereas force fields predicting different inner/outer binding equilibria remain largely indistinguishable at the current experimental resolution. These results highlight the dominant role of sampling in modelling divalent ion binding and provide a general strategy for integrating simulations with experimental data in complex biomolecular systems. | | | | --- | --- | | Comments: | Main text: 25 pages (5 figures); Supplementary Information: 24 pages (14 figures, 3 tables) | | Subjects: | Biomolecules (q-bio.BM); Biological Physics (physics.bio-ph); Computational Physics (physics.comp-ph) | | Cite as: | [arXiv:2607.07186](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07186) \[q-bio.BM\] | | | (or [arXiv:2607.07186v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07186v1) \[q-bio.BM\] for this version) | | | [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.07186](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.07186)

title: Equivalence testing in pesticide risk assessment -- Evaluation and practical guidance for design, analysis and interpretation
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07543#content) ![](https://arxiv.org/static/base/1.0.1/images/icons/smileybones-small.svg)arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! [Learn more](https://info.arxiv.org/about) × Search arXiv Press Enter to search · [Advanced search](https://arxiv.org/search/advanced) # Statistics > Methodology **arXiv:2607.07543** (stat) \[Submitted on 8 Jul 2026\] # Title:Equivalence testing in pesticide risk assessment -- Evaluation and practical guidance for design, analysis and interpretation Authors: [Dimitry Wintermantel](https://arxiv.org/search/stat?searchtype=author&query=Wintermantel,+D), [Julia Osterman](https://arxiv.org/search/stat?searchtype=author&query=Osterman,+J), [Magdalena M. Mair](https://arxiv.org/search/stat?searchtype=author&query=Mair,+M+M), [Florian Hartig](https://arxiv.org/search/stat?searchtype=author&query=Hartig,+F) View a PDF of the paper titled Equivalence testing in pesticide risk assessment -- Evaluation and practical guidance for design, analysis and interpretation, by Dimitry Wintermantel and 2 other authors [View PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.07543) > Abstract:Harmful pesticide effects exceeding specific protection goals (SPG) may go undetected in underpowered experimental designs. Regulatory honeybee field studies have consistently failed to reach the statistical power required under European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) guidance, which may have caused approval of high-risk substances. Therefore, EFSA advised a shift from testing the null hypothesis of 'no effect' to equivalence testing. Under this approach, a pesticide is classified as 'low risk' if the null hypothesis that its effect exceeds the SPG can be rejected. For honeybees, the recommended SPG is a colony size reduction below 10%. Critics have argued that this framework requires excessive site replication to demonstrate pesticide safety and proposed an alternative equivalence test defining treatment effects relative to the lower bound of the 90%-control-group confidence interval. Using simulations mimicking a regulatory honeybee field study, we show that although the two equivalence tests share the same trade-off between false 'low-risk' and false 'high-risk' classifications, only EFSA's original recommendation reliably identifies pesticides with effects > SPG at alpha = 0.2. Our results show that increasing site replication beyond the current practice is unavoidable for a reliable regulatory assessment. However, for pesticides with effect sizes of 5% or less, site requirements remain lower than those implied by the power requirement of the former EFSA guidance. Moreover, covariate adjustment through a model term or balanced colony allocation using anticlustering randomisation can reduce site requirements without losing power and thus save costs. Finally, we provide guidance and R functions for anticlustering randomisation and equivalence testing for pesticide risk assessment. | | | | --- | --- | | Comments: | 39 pages, 12 figures, 1 table | | Subjects: | Methodology (stat.ME); Populations and Evolution (q-bio.PE); Quantitative Methods (q-bio.QM) | | Cite as: | [arXiv:2607.07543](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07543) \[stat.ME\] | | | (or [arXiv:2607.07543v1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.07543v1) \[stat.ME\] for this version) | | | [https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.07543](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2607.07543)
